items.title: TOAST Classification
items.contentHtml:
TOAST classification of ischemic stroke

  • TOAST divides into 5 subtypes: atherothrombotic (LAA), cardioembolic (CE), lacunar (SVD), other determined cause (ODE) and undetermined cause (SUE).
  • Atherothrombotic: stenosis ≥50% or thrombosis of a large artery; requires Doppler or CT angiography.
  • Cardioembolic: presence of AF, valve prosthesis, recent AMI or LV thrombus; high recurrence.
  • Lacunar: small infarction (<15 mm) in perforating territory, associated with chronic HTN.
  • Other cause: dissection, vasculitis, thrombophilia; requires directed studies.
  • Indeterminate: two possible causes or incomplete study; up to 30% of cases.

items.title: IV thrombolysis window
items.contentHtml:
Therapeutic window for IV thrombolysis

  • The standard window is 4.5 hours from the onset of symptoms or last healthy moment.
  • Between 3 and 4.5 hours apply ECASS III criteria: age >80 years, oral anticoagulants (INR ≤1.7), diabetes + previous stroke are exclusions relatives.
  • In stroke upon awakening or unknown time, use diffusion-FLAIR mismatch MRI (DWI positive, FLAIR negative) for window up to 4.5 hours from discovery if they meet WAKE-UP criteria.
  • Dose alteplase: 0.9 mg/kg (max 90 mg), 10% in bolus and the rest in infusion 1 h.
  • Do not exceed window or use beyond 4.5 hours without advanced image.

items.title: Alteplase exclusions
items.contentHtml:
Exclusion criteria for alteplase

  • Absolute: intracranial hemorrhage on CT, suspected SAH, history of ICH, major surgery or head trauma in <3 months, active bleeding.
  • Relative: INR >1.7, aPTT >40 s, platelets <100,000, glycemia <50 or >400 mg/dL, AMI in <3 months, recent major surgery.
  • BP: SBP >185 mmHg or DBP >110 mmHg maintained despite treatment is exclusion.
  • Minor non-disabling stroke (NIHSS 0-4) and rapid improvement: they are not absolute exclusion if there is a deficit disabling.
  • Pregnancy is relative; individual risk/benefit.

items.title: NIHSS
items.contentHtml:
NIHSS scale and its interpretation

  • NIHSS (National Institutes of Health Stroke Scale): 15 items, score 0-42; <5 mild, 5-15 moderate, 16-20 moderate-severe, >20 severe.
  • Key items: level of consciousness (1a,1b,1c), conjugate gaze (2), visual field (3), facial paralysis (4), motor MS (5) and MI (6), ataxia (7), sensitivity (8), language (9), dysarthria (10), extinction (11).
  • NIHSS ≥6 usually indicates large vessel occlusion candidate for endovascular therapy.
  • Interpretation: each point increases the odds of a bad outcome; NIHSS >25 associates high risk of hemorrhagic transformation with thrombolysis.
  • It is performed on admission, 24 hours and at discharge to evaluate evolution.

items.title: Endovascular therapy
items.contentHtml:
Endovascular therapy with stent retriever

  • Indication: occlusion of proximal anterior circulation (ICA, M1, M2) with NIHSS ≥6 and window up to 6 hours from onset (up to 24 h with mismatch perfusion in DAWN/DEFUSE-3 studies).
  • Procedure: stent retriever (e.g. Solitaire, Trevo) is deployed in the thrombus, left for 5 minutes and removed with simultaneous aspiration.
  • Efficacy: TICI 2b-3 (complete reperfusion) in >70% of cases; reduces disability (mRS ≤2 to 90 days).
  • Does not require perfusion imaging test if <6 h; if >6 h, use CTP or perfusion MRI for mismatch.
  • Contraindications: established infarction >1/3 MCA territory on plain CT, hemorrhage, NIHSS <6.

items.foco: Management of blood pressure in the acute phase
items.title: Management of acute BP
items.contentHtml:
Management of blood pressure in the acute phase

  • If candidate for thrombolysis: maintain SBP <185 mmHg and DBP <110 mmHg before alteplase; If it exceeds, use labetalol 10-20 mg IV or nicardipine 5 mg/h.
  • Without thrombolysis: permissiveness up to SBP ≤220 mmHg and DBP ≤120 mmHg in the first 24 hours, except for comorbidity (aortic dissection, PAD).
  • If thrombolysis is started: maintain SBP <180 mmHg and DBP <105 mmHg during 24 hours post-infusion.
  • Avoid sudden decrease (>15% in 24 hours) due to risk of ischemic penumbra.
  • Use titratable IV drugs: labetalol, nicardipine, clevidipine.

items.foco: Differential diagnosis of stroke vs. seizure
items.title: DDx stroke vs seizure
items.contentHtml:
Differential diagnosis of stroke vs. seizure

  • Postictal seizure (Todd's palsy): focal deficit that resolves in <24 h, typically <1 h; EEG shows epileptiform activity.
  • Stroke: sudden deficit without tonic-clonic movements, with signs of lateralization; image (CT/MRI) shows acute infarction.
  • Clues: seizure at the beginning (suggests epilepsy), tongue biting, intense headache (SAH), versive eye movements.
  • MRI with diffusion is gold standard: restriction in acute stroke; normal in Todd's palsy.
  • If in doubt, perform urgent EEG and do not delay thrombolysis if stroke is highly suspected.

items.title: Antiplatelet in minor stroke
items.contentHtml:
Antiplatelet treatment in minor stroke

  • Minor stroke: NIHSS ≤3; acute management with AAS 300 mg/day orally started within 24-48 h post-stroke if thrombolysis was not administered.
  • CHANCE and POINT study: dual therapy AAS + clopidogrel (loading 300 mg, then 75 mg/day) reduces recurrence at 90 days in minor or stroke High-risk TIA (ABCD2 ≥4).
  • Duration: 21 days in CHANCE; in POINT up to 90 days but with greater bleeding.
  • Contraindication: intracranial hemorrhage, allergy, high risk of bleeding.
  • Do not use antiplatelet therapy if thrombolysis was given; wait 24 hours post-alteplase.

items.title: Cardioembolic anticoagulation
items.contentHtml:
Indications for anticoagulation in cardioembolic conditions

  • Cardioembolic due to non-valvular atrial fibrillation (AF): initiate oral anticoagulation (OAC) with NOAC (apixaban, rivaroxaban, edoxaban, dabigatran) instead of warfarin (INR 2-3).
  • Start: rule 1-3-6-12: OAC after 24 hours if TIA, 3 days if small infarction, 6 days if moderate, 12 days if large (NIHSS >15 or >1/3 MCA).
  • If valvular AF (mechanical prosthesis, moderate-severe mitral stenosis): use warfarin (INR 2.5-3.5).
  • Do not start OAC if hemorrhagic transformation or uncontrolled hypertension.
  • In LV thrombus or AMI with aneurysm: anticoagulation with warfarin or heparin according to guidelines.

items.title: Anterior vs posterior circulation
items.contentHtml:
Anterior circulation stroke vs. posterior

  • Anterior circulation (ACI, MCA, ACA): contralateral hemiparesis/hemihypoesthesia, aphasia (dominant hemisphere), hemineglect (non-dominant), gaze deviation.
  • Posterior circulation (vertebral, basilar, PCA, PICA, AICA): vertigo, diplopia, ataxia, dysphagia, Horner, homonymous hemianopia, alteration of lower cranial nerves.
  • Sign of posterior malignancy: basilar occlusion with tetraparesis, coma, vertical gaze palsy; mortality >80% without treatment.
  • Image: CT angiography of supra-aortic trunks for anterior; posterior circulation CT angiography or MRA for vertebrobasilar.
  • Endovascular therapy in basilar: window up to 24 hours with mismatch perfusion.

items.foco: Signs of malignancy in cerebral infarction
items.title: Signs of malignancy
items.contentHtml:
Signs of malignancy in cerebral infarction

  • Malignant MCA infarction: >50% of the MCA territory on CT or MRI, with massive cerebral edema and midline shift >5 mm.
  • Clinical criteria: NIHSS >20 in dominant hemisphere or >15 in non-dominant hemisphere, impaired level of consciousness in the first 48 h.
  • Insular ribbon sign erased on CT (insula hypodensity) is an early predictor of malignancy.
  • Treatment: decompressive craniectomy within 48 hours reduces mortality (DECIMAL, DESTINY, HAMLET studies).
  • Age <60 years and surgery before 48 hours improves prognosis. functional.

items.title: Secondary prevention
items.contentHtml:
Secondary prevention: antiplatelet agents and statins

  • Antiplatelet therapy: AAS 75-100 mg/day or clopidogrel 75 mg/day as monotherapy; in minor stroke use AAS + clopidogrel for 21 days.
  • If cardioembolic: NOAC (dose adjusted to renal function and weight) or warfarin.
  • Statins: atorvastatin 80 mg/day (SPARCL study) reduces recurrence regardless of baseline LDL; LDL target <70 mg/dL or reduction ≥50%.
  • Management of risk factors: HBP (<130/80 mmHg), DM (HbA1c <7%), smoking cessation, exercise.
  • Prolonged double antiplatelet therapy (>90 days) not recommended due to bleeding risk.
